print_settings.supports: no — straight cylindrical walls, no overhangs
print_settings.orientation: flat bottom face on print bed (upright)

key_decisions: Used boolean subtraction (outer minus inner cylinder) instead of shell() — more reliable for a simple cylindrical form and avoids OCC shell fragility.
Wall thickness set to 3mm (above 1.2mm minimum) because water bowls are handled daily and need to resist flex, drops, and dishwasher cycles.
Floor thickness 3mm matches wall thickness for a uniform cross-section, reducing differential shrinkage and warping in PETG.
Chamfer on bottom outer edge (1.5mm) instead of fillet — avoids elephant foot without requiring supports, keeps the base flat for bed adhesion.
Dimensions sourced from published small-dog bowl products (150mm diameter, 65mm height) to match real-world scale for a toy/small breed.
Tapered frustum (cone) shape: base 104mm dia, top 154mm dia — gives elegant chalice silhouette and matches user request for narrow base that lips upward.
Foot disc (120mm dia, 7mm tall) wider than bowl base (104mm): creates an 8mm visible ledge/lip at the base junction.
Dog bone raised emboss built as two cylinders + box on YZ plane, extruded radially in +X, penetrating 0.3mm into wall for solid boolean union — protrudes 1.5mm above surface.
Chamfer applied to outer frustum before boolean cut (not after): OCC chamfer fails on post-cut annular top ring edges with tapered walls.
Bone contour re-extracted using skimage.measure.find_contours (marching squares) — replaces broken angle-sort method that produced 79 self-intersecting jumps on the non-convex bone waist.
Engraving (boolean subtract) chosen over raised emboss: no overhangs, self-supporting, cleaner print.
Bone sized 60mm wide × 24.8mm tall, 1.5mm deep — legible on a 154mm bowl while leaving 1.5mm of wall above the minimum 1.2mm threshold.
Image contour extraction workflow now documented: save reference image to assets/, run find_contours(), normalize, save polygon_json — no manual geometry.

print_settings.supports: none — vertical walls only, bone pocket is horizontal

key_decisions: Moved bone engraving from exterior curved wall to interior flat floor — eliminates surface-projection math and distortion entirely.
Bone sized to 70% of inner diameter (70.6mm wide × 29.2mm tall, aspect ratio preserved from polygon) — fills floor visually without crowding the walls.
Pocket depth 1.5mm into 3.5mm floor — leaves 2.0mm of material, well above 1.2mm printability minimum.
lessons_learned: Engraving a flat polygon onto a curved frustum wall via YZ-plane extrude creates a distortion artifact: only the narrow cross-section where the flat solid meets the curved surface is visible, producing a crescent/smile shape instead of the intended design. Fix: always place decorative engravings on flat planar surfaces (interior floor, flat rim) rather than curved exterior walls.

print_settings.orientation: Base: open side up. Lid: skirt-down on bed (bone emboss prints last, top quality) — no supports needed either way.

key_decisions: Lid built as two-piece union: top plate (same outer footprint, 2mm thick) + hollow skirt tube (skirt_outer = base_inner − 2×tolerance = 75.6×55.6mm, depth = lid_h − wall_t = 6mm). Skirt is boolean subtraction, not shell.
Skirt outer fillet capped at 1mm (below wall_t=2mm) to avoid thin-wall geometry at corners.
Lid plate overhang over skirt is 2.2mm/side — prints without supports.
Dog bone emboss reused from dog_water_bowl assets at scale 0.45 (45×18.6mm), raised 1mm proud on flat lid top.
Tolerance 0.2mm/side for PLA friction fit on Prusa MK4S.
lessons_learned: Friction-fit lip / stackable peg pattern: skirt_outer = base_inner − 2×tolerance; skirt_depth = lid_h − wall_t. For stackable objects, add the same skirt geometry to the BOTTOM of each tier so it pegs into the open top of the tier below. Same tolerance and fillet values apply.

print_settings.orientation: Base: open side up. Lid: skirt-down on bed — no supports needed either way.

key_decisions: Fixed SKIRT_R from v1's conservative 1mm to correct formula SKIRT_R = INNER_R - TOLERANCE = 2.8mm — eliminates visible corner mismatch and interference between skirt and base cavity corners.
All other geometry unchanged from v1 — only the skirt corner fillet radius corrected.
lessons_learned: Skirt fillet formula SKIRT_R = INNER_R - TOLERANCE (2.8mm) confirmed correct for friction fit. Conservative 1mm caused skirt corners to be visibly more rectangular than the base inner cavity, creating interference at corners.

print_settings.supports: none — open cylinder, inner step faces upward
print_settings.orientation: flat bottom on bed, open top facing up

key_decisions: Retaining lip formed by stepped inner bore: main bore 108.3mm ID (bottom 93.6mm), then lip bore 92.3mm ID (top 8mm) — creates an 8mm-wide annular shelf at Z=93.6mm that the bowl base rests on.
Fit clearance set to 1.0mm (generous) per user request to account for measurement error — lip opening 92.3mm accommodates bowl bases up to ~90mm.
Chamfer applied to outer bottom edge of the solid cylinder BEFORE boolean cuts — applying after cuts selects inner bore edges too and causes OCC failure.
Open top and bottom (no floor) — easy to clean, structurally sufficient as the 3mm walls carry all load.
lessons_learned: Chamfer on bottom outer edge after stepped-bore boolean cuts fails with OCC error because the selector picks both outer and inner bottom edges. Fix: apply chamfer to the outer cylinder primitive before performing any boolean cuts.

print_settings.supports: none — transition slopes at 52 deg from horizontal
print_settings.orientation: flat bottom on bed, socket facing up

key_decisions: Corrected design concept: bowl drops INTO a socket (v1 had wrong concept — internal shelf the bowl sat on top of). Socket walls wrap around the bowl exterior to retain it.
Three-section outer solid: pedestal cylinder (88mm OD) + tapered frustum transition + socket ring (127mm OD). Two-section loft used only for the frustum.
Transition frustum angle 52 deg from horizontal (pedestal 88mm → socket 127mm over 25mm height) — above 45 deg threshold, no supports needed.
Inner bores as two separate straight cylinders: pedestal bore (82mm ID, 0→67mm) + socket bore (119mm ID, 67→101.6mm). The annular shelf at Z=67mm supports the bowl.
Chamfer applied to pedestal primitive before union operations (v1 lesson preserved).
lessons_learned: Always confirm the physical interaction model before designing: 'does the item sit ON TOP, drop INTO, or clip ONTO?' Wrong concept cannot be fixed by tuning dimensions — it requires a full redesign.
User sketches are the authoritative spec and override text descriptions. Read the sketch for the interaction model first.
Always read intent_spec.json before asking clarifying questions — the bowl OD (114.3mm) was already recorded in the spec and should not have been re-asked during the redesign discussion.

print_settings.orientation: base flat on bed, spine standing upright

key_decisions: Spine built as YZ cross-section extrusion (parallelogram) instead of box+rotate — eliminates watertight issues from spine-base gap that appear after rotation.
Spine embedded 2mm into base (starts at Z=base_h-2) for guaranteed volumetric overlap, ensuring clean boolean union.

MagSafe is a through-hole (56.5mm dia): puck inserted from rear, held by phone magnets. Recess approach failed — horizontal cylinder on tilted face only opens the upper half-circle; lower arc is a hidden internal void.
Cable slot (5mm wide) cuts straight through spine+base from puck-hole bottom, overlapping puck hole by 1mm to avoid tangent-edge OCC boolean failure.
front_clearance = 12mm (phone_t + case margin); rear_clearance = 30mm — phone CG ~19mm rearward from spine, stand is anti-tip stable.
lessons_learned: Horizontal cylinder cut into a tilted face creates only a half-circle opening — the lower arc is a hidden internal void. Always use through-holes for charger cutouts on tilted panels.
Cable slot must overlap the puck hole by at least 1mm (not tangent) to avoid OCC boolean failure on the shared edge.
Cut cable slots from the combined base+spine result, not from the spine alone — the base union seals the embedded overlap region and closes any spine-only cuts.

print_settings.supports: none — flare self-supporting (narrows going up); transition at 52 deg
print_settings.orientation: flat flare base on bed, socket facing up

key_decisions: Solid flared base: 150mm OD → 88mm OD over 20mm — ~70% more footprint than v2, mass concentrated at base lowers CoG.
Flare section kept solid (no hollow) — maximises base mass for stability, simplifies geometry.
Pedestal height reduced from 101.6mm to 68.9mm so total height decreases by exactly 0.5in (12.7mm) to 131.2mm.
Chamfer applied to flare primitive using faces('<Z').edges().chamfer() — same pattern as cylinder, works correctly on loft frustum bottom face.
lessons_learned: Anti-tip flared base: solid frustum wider at bottom tapering to pedestal OD over 20mm provides ~70% more footprint, lowers CoG, adds no overhang risk. Keep flare solid, deduct flare height from pedestal height to control total model height.

print_settings.orientation: flat on one 25.4mm (1in) Y-face; XZ profile stands vertical; add 5mm brim

key_decisions: S/Z extruded profile: both top arm and lower arm extend in the same direction (toward desk interior), not opposite. This keeps the backpack weight pulling the hook into the desk edge rather than tipping it forward.
Stop tab at far end of lower arm: 15mm tall upturned retainer built into the 2D profile polygon — no separate union needed.
3mm fillets applied only to |Y edges (extrusion-direction edges) — avoids complex OCC fillet failures on post-union topology while still giving a soft rounded look.
Uniform 8mm wall thickness throughout for load-bearing integrity in PLA under backpack weight.
lessons_learned: Gravity-lock desk hook: both arms must extend the same direction (toward desk interior) so backpack weight presses the hook INTO the desk edge — not away from it. Opposite arms create a tipping moment.
S/Z profile extruded from XZ workplane is the cleanest approach for complex 2D profiles — single solid body, clean topology, reliable filleting vs union-of-boxes.

print_settings.orientation: lay flat on one 25.4mm (1in) Y-face; the 己/Z profile stands vertical => 0% overhangs; add 5mm brim (tall, narrow footprint)

key_decisions: Corrected the profile to a folded-ribbon 己/Z per the user's side-view sketch: the counterbalance TOP ARM extends backward (toward desk interior) while the CRADLE extends FORWARD (toward/past the desk edge) — OPPOSITE directions, spine on the interior (left) side. This supersedes v1/v2's 'both arms same direction' assumption.
Mid lip extended to the full top-arm length (50.8mm) so the desk is clamped over its full span — removes the cantilever that made v2/v3.0 look unstable.
Spine relocated to the interior (left) end; both grip arms run to the mouth wall, so the cradle (63.5mm) ends just 12.7mm past the mouth — 'a bit further' per the user.

Walls reduced 8->6mm uniform after a load check: the cradle is the only real load path and, printed on-side, its bending stress runs along the layers (not across them) -> SF ~1.4 for a normal bag at 6mm, ~27% less material.
Stop tab shortened 15->10mm; thickness kept = wall (6mm) for consistent thickness per user request.
Fillet reduced 3->2mm: a 3mm fillet overlaps on both parallel edges of a 6mm-wide face (OCC BRep failure).
Built as overlapping Y-extruded boxes (every fold overlaps its neighbour) -> verified ONE shell: Solids==1, watertight, Euler==2, mesh splits to 1 body.
lessons_learned: A user's side-view sketch OVERRIDES prior text assumptions. The desk hook is a folded ribbon (己/Z): the load arm (cradle) extends FORWARD past the desk edge, OPPOSITE the counterbalance top arm — NOT the same direction claimed by v1's lesson. Verify fold direction against the sketch before coding.

'One continuous piece' failures come from members that only touch (share a face) instead of overlapping. Make every fold volumetrically overlap its neighbour and assert solids()==1 / watertight / Euler==2.
Max fillet radius on a member filleted on BOTH parallel edges of a face is just under half the face width: 3mm fails on a 6mm wall; use 2mm.

The standard overhang formula counts the flat bed face as a 90deg overhang. For a constant-section prism printed on its extrusion-axis face, real overhangs are 0 — verify by excluding bed-contact (y≈ymin) faces.

print_settings.orientation: lay flat on one 25.4mm Y-face; 己/Z profile stands vertical => 0% overhangs; add 5mm brim

key_decisions: Minor refinement of v3: stop-tab height 10 -> 7.5mm (3/4) for a lower catch. All other geometry identical to v3; still one continuous shell (Solids==1, watertight).
lessons_learned: Tab height is independent of overall model height here — the tab top (z = wall_t + tab_h = 13.5mm) sits well below the top arm (74.75mm), so shrinking it does not change the bounding box.

object: Hollow 'club' / water-tower shaped vessel that houses a 25GA round DC gear motor
verdict: DELIVERED (proof of concept; formal design review skipped at user request)

print_settings.supports: tree supports under the head underside overhang (user adds in PrusaSlicer); cap needs none
print_settings.orientation: body: open base flat on bed, head up; cap: flat side down

key_decisions: Shaft widened from 1in (25.4mm) to 31-33mm so a 25mm-dia 25GA/25D motor fits inside (1in bore would have been ~21mm, too small).
Water-tower orientation: shaft is the base (open bottom), head on top and sealed. Motor inserts from the bottom and lives at the bottom of the shaft.

Separate base cap retains the motor (bolts to its 2x M3 face holes @ 17mm), provides drive-shaft exit (center) and wire pass-through (side); spigot press-fits into the 27mm bore with 0.4mm diametral clearance.
Body built as cone(shaft) UNION oblate-ellipsoid(head), junction filleted, then hollowed via boolean cut (shell() failed on this geometry).
lessons_learned: Bumps on a hollow shell: union the bump spheres to the SOLID body BEFORE hollowing, and seat each bump center INSIDE the cavity wall (a few mm), so the cavity cut passes through solid sphere meat instead of grazing the sphere edge tangentially (tangent cuts => degenerate slivers, non-watertight).

A bump sphere placed on a revolve's apex breaks watertightness because the sphere primitive's tessellation pole coincides with the revolve axis pole. Rotate the bump sphere 90deg (cq.Solid.makeSphere(r).rotate((0,0,0),(1,0,0),90)) to move its mesh pole off-axis; sink it slightly for a transversal merge.

shell() (BRep_API: command not done) and two-independent-spline outer/inner profiles (splines diverge and pinch the wall into multiple solids) both fail for a bulb-on-column. Reliable path: clean primitives -> union -> fillet junction -> boolean-cut a cavity built from matching primitives.
Fillet the cone/ellipsoid neck by selecting just the intersection circle with a BoxSelector at z = ellipsoid_center - ellipsoid_semi_v (where the cone side exits the ellipsoid surface).

object: clipper blade organizer (v1b: 2x7 grid, flat, tilted slots, thin walls)

print_settings.supports: no -- 8deg slots are well within printable overhang

key_decisions: 2 columns x 7 rows = 14 cells; 2 boxes = 28 blades (15-30 range)
THIN walls: wall/divider 3.0/2.5 -> 2.0/2.0, base 4 -> 2.5 (much less material)
Slots are CUT at an 8deg tilt (not faked in the preview) so a real blade reclines and its stamp angles toward the viewer; previous v1b cut vertical pockets and only leaned the proxy blades

Scalloped WAVE top matching the reference: one wide cylinder per COLUMN (axis along Y, R from target dip+chord) dips every divider + front/back wall AT EACH CELL CENTRE, leaving peaks at the spine and outer walls. Replaces the earlier flat front lip + lowered spine, which read as a junk lip and a centre gap (both removed).
114x162x24mm; two side-by-side (240mm) fit the drawer; depth set to 24mm per user (deeper cradle, still under the 26mm reference)
Approved by an INDEPENDENT cad-reviewer agent at 20mm (rendered big, verified the wave/walls/tilt on the actual mesh); its only reservation was shallow depth, resolved by the user choosing 24mm.
lessons_learned: A repeating scallop/wave is a dip PER CELL: cut a cylinder ACROSS the dividers at each cell centre (axis spanning the depth). A single cylinder ALONG a divider only lowers it uniformly -> no wave (this exact bug shipped and the user caught it). R = (chord^2/4 + dip^2)/(2*dip), centre Z = wall_h + (R - dip).

If a part is meant to hold something at a tilt, CUT the slot at that tilt -- don't lean only the preview proxy. Use blade_proxy() oversized as the CUTTER (same recline as the seated blade) so slot and part match.

Render BIG (single subject, reference side-by-side via review_render.py) and have an INDEPENDENT reviewer judge it before presenting -- the 4-panel thumbnails are too small once downscaled, which is how a missing scallop slipped past a green numeric gate.

printer: Prusa XL (287mm deep; too deep for MK4S)

print_settings.orientation: lay on BACK so reclined slots are near-vertical -> no supports

key_decisions: Same 2x5 = 10-blade grid; blades reclined 27deg above horizontal -> blade top 3.5mm below rim -> stacks 2-high; stack=93.6mm fits 101.6mm drawer
2-column layout keeps all 10 blades despite reclining (recline spreads rows only)

WIDE-blade reliefs: central divider is a low 12mm rib + left/right walls dropped to 30mm over the blade lanes so wide heads overhang inward AND outward; front/back walls + 4 corner posts stay full height
Registration: 4 SOLID corner posts carry the nubs + blind holes (user likes the nubs); fixes prior floating-nub bug
Constraint: don't seat wide blades back-to-back in the same row of both columns (heads meet at centre) -> stagger wides
lessons_learned: Reclining for stack height spreads the row axis only; a 2-column grid preserves capacity
Wide blades overhang ~8mm each side of a standard cell -> need relief BOTH at the central divider and the outer walls, not just one
Registration nubs must sit on solid posts, not a thin perimeter rim, or they float over the cavity

object: clipper blade organizer (v3: 2x5 flat grid, prototype 2)
verdict: APPROVED (lead self-review, parameter-only modify edit; flat-grid build clean, watertight). Gate's stacked_height/blade_below_rim are stale stackable-concept measurements, N/A to this flat grid.

print_settings.walls: 4 (~1.6mm; bump to fully fill the 2mm walls)
print_settings.orientation: flat on bed, base down (6deg slot walls are within overhang)

key_decisions: Prototype 2: shrank 2x7->2x5 (14->10 cells) for ~28% less material / faster print; matches the reference layout and fits the drawer two-wide (240x112mm).
Reduced slot tilt 8->6deg (user request); side benefit: front-wall tilt wedge shrank from ~4.2mm to ~2.8mm and back-wall breach risk dropped further.
Raised wall_h 24->25mm: pocket 21.5->24.5mm (~48% blade capture) for a bit more stability with the more-upright blades.
Kept the v2 back-wall interior clamp (cutter.intersect(interior)) so the lean can't breach the 2mm perimeter walls.
Scaled review to the change: parameter-only edit => lead self-review (render+gate+contains() wall probes), no cold cad-reviewer.
lessons_learned: Reducing slot tilt reduces the unavoidable front-wall wedge (material behind the lean): 8deg->~4.2mm, 6deg->~2.8mm at the base.
A captured pocket (>=~43% of blade height, low side clearance) won't let a teeth-up blade tip out even with CoM above the rim; it only rocks ~±8deg before the slot walls arrest it.
rtree must be installed for trimesh contains()/signed_distance; without it wall-solidity probes silently fall back to flaky section-counting.

print_settings.orientation: standing on a 28x67.6 mm Y-end face; all walls vertical, no supports

key_decisions: Rebuilt clean-room (no v4 peeking) after v1 regressed (bulky, no fillets, 40mm lever).
Compact proportions derived from FUNCTION: strap near edge (14mm), reaction arm 38mm (lever 0.37), width 28mm.
Built by extruding ONE mitered outline (ribbon_outline) -> single body -> .clean()+2mm fillet works (fix-don't-drop).
lessons_learned: Intrinsic quality gate (print_checks) + fix-don't-drop produced a GREAT part first build: blueprint pre-screen, numeric gate, print_checks, reviewer all passed on iteration 1.
Held to a high bar with NO reference; after-the-fact it matched v4 (58.8x28x67.6 vs 63.5x25.4x74.8mm) which had needed 4 versions + human sketch fixes.
Overhang metric must exclude bed-contact faces or it false-flags a profile prism's footprint.

verdict: APPROVED (lead self-review: parameter-only modify, no topology change)

print_settings.infill: raise as needed for load (volume already low)
print_settings.orientation: standing on a 24x73.6 mm Y-end face; all walls vertical

key_decisions: v2 modify of v1 (no override): shorter lip (rise 12.7->6.1mm), bigger strap cradle (drop 26->34, throat ~14->~26mm; reach 14->18), less bulky (wall 6->4, width 28->24). Slot unchanged 35.6mm.
Volume -41% (36965->21637mm3); user will raise infill instead of wall thickness for strength.
Fix-don't-drop: fillet 1.5 made the 4mm-wall mesh non-watertight at the tight mouth/lip corners; reduced to 1.0mm (clean, still rounded) rather than dropping fillets.

Lip reshaped (override) from a thin leaning hook to a SUBTLE solid triangular wedge: flat vertical outer face flush with the shelf front, short inner ramp (~41 deg) so the strap rides up a smooth catch -- matches the user's sketch. Built as a unioned wedge on the ribbon (true local feature), not a ribbon segment.
lessons_learned: On thin (4mm) walls a fillet >= ~wall/3 can collide at tight corners and break watertightness; pick the largest radius that stays manifold, don't drop the feature.
Parameter-only modify -> lead self-review (preview + gate + print_checks + v1->v2 regression diff), no cold reviewer, per Board scale-the-review rule.
